Case Study
Scroll
01

Overview

UI Design Studio is a centralised portal for managing and distributing reusable UI assets — templates, design stencils, icons, components, and themes — built for HCL Technologies' internal delivery teams.

The core insight was simple: every project was rebuilding the same UI from scratch. By creating a shared, searchable library with project management, contribution workflows, and advanced search, teams could start with reviewed, cross-browser compatible components instead of blank canvases.

The platform covers three distinct experiences — a public-facing portal for discovery and downloads, an admin console for full library management, and a contributor console for community-sourced additions — all unified under a single design system.

Platform PHP / Web Application
Portals Public, Admin, Contributor
Asset types Templates, Stencils, Icons, Themes, Components
Frameworks jQuery, Bootstrap, Backbone, Knockout
Releases Alpha · Beta · Final
Effort saving 40–60% vs traditional development
02

Features

Discovery

Advanced asset search

Search templates and components by business domain, browser support, platform, form factor, responsive design flag, and category — not just by name.

Library

Ready-to-use component repo

Pre-reviewed, cross-browser compatible UI components and templates built on Bootstrap, jQuery, Backbone, and Knockout — ready to download and customise immediately.

Projects

Project-scoped asset management

Teams can manage project-specific templates, themes, design stencils, and components — keeping assets organised per delivery rather than shared in one undifferentiated pool.

Contribution

Community contribution workflow

Any user can register as a contributor and submit templates, icons, and themes. Admin approval gates ensure quality before assets enter the shared library.

Responsive

RWD-ready components

All templates and components are built to Responsive Web Design standards — tested across Android and iOS, tablet and phone, before being published to the library.

Tracking

Download & usage tracking

Inbuilt download tracking, usage analytics, feedback loops, and support ticket systems give admins visibility into which assets are actually being used — and what's missing.

03

System Design

01

Public Site

Discovery, search, login, downloads and contributor profiles.

02

Admin Console

Full resource management with role-based access control.

03

Contributor Console

Limited admin view for managing personal contributions.

Public · Dashboard
Logged-in Dashboard
  • Featured templates carousel with case study links
  • My Projects panel — direct access to team assets
  • Library quick-search and contributor CTA
Public · Home
Public Home Page
  • Featured template listings, login widget, news
  • Browse by categories, contributor profiles
  • User registration and contact
Public · Post-Login
User Dashboard
  • Project management hub
  • Library search and support ticket creation
  • Contributor sign-up and case study browsing
Admin · Dashboard
Admin Console
  • Notifications for pending approval requests
  • Template, stencil, icon, theme, component tabs
Admin · Management
Template Edit & Approval
  • Edit template metadata, themes, domains, platforms
  • HTML/PSD file upload and download tracking
  • Manage users, projects, registrations globally
Contributor · Console
Contributor Console
  • Limited admin view — manages only personal contributions
  • Upload and manage templates, stencils, icons, components, themes
  • Profile management — separate from full admin privileges
04

Efficiency Gains

Traditional UI delivery requires every project to run usability analysis, visual design, and HTML/CSS development from scratch — typically 24 days for 5 screens.

UI Design Studio inverts this: teams begin with ready-to-use, reviewed components and spend time only on customisation — collapsing the same workload to approximately 10 days.

24d
Traditional development — 5 screens
10d
UI Design Studio — same scope
60%
Maximum effort saving
40%
Minimum effort saving
05

Benefits

01

Faster web development

Starting from reviewed, tested components eliminates the most time-intensive phases of any UI project — analysis, design, and initial build.

02

Centralised searchable repository

Templates, icons, UI components, and visual designs — all in one place, searchable by domain, platform, browser, and form factor. No more hunting across shared drives.

03

Less customisation overhead

Well-documented, structured HTML and CSS code means developers spend less time reverse-engineering someone else's code and more time adapting it to the project at hand.

04

Consistent UI across applications

Shared components mean shared design language. Projects built from the same library naturally align — improving quality without requiring design reviews at every handoff.

05

Community-driven growth

The contributor model means the library improves over time without central bottlenecks — teams that build something useful can share it, and the platform gets better with use.

06

RWD & cross-browser ready

Every component is tested against multi-device and multi-browser requirements before publication — teams inherit that testing rather than repeating it project by project.

07

Reflections

What worked

  • The efficiency comparison as the anchor. Reducing the conversation to "24 days vs 10 days" gave stakeholders a single, undeniable number to rally behind — no ambiguity about the value proposition.
  • Role-based portal split. Separating Public, Admin, and Contributor experiences rather than collapsing them into one surface kept each audience's workflow clean and reduced scope creep.
  • Admin approval gate for contributions. Building review into the contribution flow from day one protected library quality — the alternative (open contributions) would have degraded the asset value over time.
  • Phased release with visible milestones. Alpha → Beta → Final gave the team a clear delivery narrative and let stakeholders see progress without waiting for a complete product.

What I'd do differently

  • Search as Alpha priority, not WIP. The core value of the library is findability — shipping without search complete undermines the entire product's first impression.
  • Usage analytics from day one. Without tracking which assets are actually downloaded and used, it's impossible to know what to build next or whether the library is delivering value in practice.
  • Contributor onboarding flow. The contribution model relies on community participation — but a friction-heavy upload process suppresses contributions. A guided submission wizard would have accelerated library growth.
  • Define a north-star metric earlier. "Effort saved per project" is compelling but hard to measure without baseline data. Establishing tracking at Alpha would have enabled a concrete impact story at Final.
